wrong_reasoning: Positive peripheral blood smear, massive splenomegaly, anemia, and pancytopenia in a malaria-endemic region. HMS is common and the clinical picture fit the standard heuristic.
correct_reasoning: Deficiency in lysosomal enzyme glucocerebrosidase causes accumulation of glucosylceramide in macrophages, leading to hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, and skeletal changes. The Erlenmeyer flask deformity and bone marrow Gaucher cells are pathognomonic.
regional_context: Gaucher Disease is underdiagnosed in East Africa due to reliance on malaria as the primary explanation for splenomegaly. Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) is rarely available in Uganda.
key_clues: Failure to respond to multiple anti-malarial courses
Bone marrow showing wrinkled tissue paper macrophages
red_herrings: Positive malaria smear (low density co-infection)
Malaria-endemic region creating anchoring bias
Splenomegaly fitting HMS clinical picture
source: Reconstructed from published literature on Gaucher Disease in East Africa
diagnosis_delay: 2 years (recurrent admissions from age 10, diagnosed at 12)
source_title: Gaucher disease in African patients: a review of the literature and case series

wrong_reasoning: High TB burden in Cameroon, fever, weight loss, pulmonary opacities on imaging, lymphadenopathy — classic TB presentation in an endemic country.
correct_reasoning: AOSD is a rare autoinflammatory condition characterized by macrophage and neutrophil hyperactivation. The combination of quotidian fever, salmon rash, arthritis, and hyperferritinemia with glycosylated fraction <20% meets diagnostic criteria. LDH elevation reflects macrophage activation syndrome.
regional_context: AOSD is rarely considered in Central Africa where TB dominates the differential for systemic febrile illness. Extremely limited published African case series exist.
key_clues: Salmon-pink rash appearing only during fever spikes
red_herrings: Pulmonary opacities suggesting TB
High TB endemicity in Cameroon
Lymphadenopathy fitting TB picture
source: Reconstructed from published AOSD case reports in Central Africa
diagnosis_delay: 4 months (mean AOSD diagnosis delay; this case deteriorated over 4 months before correct dx)
source_title: Adult-onset Still disease: a case report from Cameroon and review of African cases

wrong_diagnosis: Primary amenorrhea secondary to nutritional deficiency / primary endocrine failure
wrong_reasoning: A 16-year-old presenting to gynecology with absent menses and suppressed FSH/LH — primary amenorrhea workup is the standard approach. Leg edema attributed to hypoalbuminemia from malnutrition.
correct_diagnosis: Type 1 Autoimmune Hepatitis (AIH) with established cirrhosis
correct_reasoning: Impaired hepatic metabolism of estrogens in cirrhosis suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, explaining delayed puberty. Silent cirrhosis presenting through the endocrine axis is a recognized but rare AIH presentation.
regional_context: AIH is vastly underdiagnosed in West Africa where viral hepatitis B dominates clinical thinking. Autoimmune serology testing is not routinely available in primary care.
key_clues: Positive ANA 1:320 in a young female
red_herrings: Primary presenting complaint to gynecology (delayed puberty)
Absence of jaundice despite advanced cirrhosis
Nutritional status explaining edema and amenorrhea
source: Reconstructed from AIH case literature in West Africa
diagnosis_delay: Several years (50% of AIH patients in South African cohort presented with established cirrhosis, indicating multi-year diagnostic delay)
source_title: Autoimmune hepatitis in South Africa: a retrospective analysis

wrong_reasoning: Reddish-purple vascular-appearing oropharyngeal mass in a non-HIV patient — KS was excluded because clinicians associated it exclusively with AIDS. Hemangioma fit the macroscopic appearance.
correct_reasoning: Endemic KS occurs in Central/East Africa independently of HIV, caused by HHV-8. Oropharyngeal location is unusual even for endemic KS. AI must not gate KS diagnosis on HIV status.
regional_context: DRC is in the HHV-8 endemic belt. Endemic KS predates the HIV epidemic and occurs in immunocompetent individuals, particularly in Central Africa.
key_clues: HIV negative — does not exclude KS in Africa
red_herrings: Three negative HIV tests creating false reassurance
Non-classic oropharyngeal location for KS
Benign macroscopic appearance
source: Reconstructed from endemic KS case reports in DRC
diagnosis_delay: 6 years median (DRC/Kivu series; range 1-38 years in HIV-negative endemic KS)
source_title: Endemic Kaposi sarcoma in HIV-negative patients in the DRC: a case series

wrong_reasoning: Chronic discharging lesion on the buttock — pilonidal cyst and hidradenitis suppurativa are common diagnoses in this region. Without grain recognition, the presentation was attributed to a common dermatological condition.
correct_reasoning: Classic chronic painless subcutaneous infection with draining sinus tracts containing black grains in the mycetoma belt. The painlessness and decade-long chronicity are characteristic. Fungal (eumycetoma) vs. bacterial (actinomycetoma) differentiation is critical for treatment (surgery vs. antibiotics).
regional_context: Somalia is in the global mycetoma belt. Madurella mycetomatis is the dominant causative fungal organism in East Africa. Highly endemic in rural farmers with barefoot soil exposure.
key_clues: Black granules in discharge — pathognomonic clue
No response to multiple antibiotic courses
red_herrings: Buttock location (vs. classic foot presentation)
Initial painless nodule resembling pilonidal cyst
Dark discharge without granule recognition
source: Reconstructed from Mycetoma case reports in the Horn of Africa
diagnosis_delay: 10 years (this specific case; median 3 years in Sudan/Somalia mycetoma series)
source_title: Mycetoma in the Horn of Africa: epidemiology, diagnosis and management challenges

wrong_reasoning: Chronic discharging foot lesion in Kassala, Sudan — in the mycetoma belt, this presentation has near-universal probability of being Madura foot. Surgical excision was planned under this assumption.
correct_reasoning: Extra-pulmonary TB can present as chronic skin ulcer without systemic symptoms. Granuloma formation on biopsy and MTB culture confirm cutaneous TB. ZN stain sensitivity is low in paucibacillary cutaneous TB — culture is essential.
regional_context: Sudan has high TB burden. Cutaneous TB is rare but documented. The mycetoma belt location creates powerful anchoring bias toward fungal etiology.
key_clues: Absence of grains in biopsy — excludes mycetoma
Culture-confirmed MTB (ZN stain can be false negative)
red_herrings: Location in mycetoma endemic belt (Kassala, Sudan)
Chronic painless foot lesion — classic mycetoma description
Normal chest X-ray reducing TB suspicion
source: Reconstructed from Omdurman Teaching Hospital CTB case literature
diagnosis_delay: 7 years (this specific case; cutaneous TB misdiagnosed as mycetoma in Kassala)
source_title: Cutaneous tuberculosis mimicking mycetoma in Sudan: a case report

wrong_reasoning: Painless, well-defined subcutaneous plaque in a child from Buruli-endemic southern Benin — the plaque stage of Buruli ulcer is clinically identical to this presentation. Geographic prevalence drove the diagnosis.
correct_reasoning: Basidiobolomycosis is a rare subcutaneous fungal infection caused by B. ranarum, a saprophyte found in decaying vegetation and soil. The Splendore-Hoeppli phenomenon on biopsy is pathognomonic. Peripheral blood hypereosinophilia (3,784/mm3) should override geographic Buruli bias.
regional_context: Basidiobolomycosis is endemic in West Africa, particularly in Benin, Nigeria, and Ghana. Often co-endemic with Buruli ulcer, creating diagnostic confusion.
red_herrings: Buruli-endemic region (southeast Benin)
Painless subcutaneous plaque in a child — classic Buruli plaque stage
Geographic prevalence of M. ulcerans overwhelming clinical reasoning
source: Reconstructed from basidiobolomycosis case reports in West Africa
diagnosis_delay: Over 1 year before referral (multiple prior misdiagnoses as Buruli ulcer)
source_title: Subcutaneous basidiobolomycosis in West Africa: case series and review

wrong_reasoning: In Somalia, periodic fever with abdominal pain in an adult is attributed to malaria or TB until extensively proven otherwise. The recurrent episodic nature was not recognized as a periodic fever syndrome.
correct_reasoning: FMF is caused by mutations in MEFV encoding pyrin, leading to IL-1β hyperproduction and sterile serositis. Episodic self-resolving fever with sterile peritonitis, erysipelas-like lesions, and family history are classic. Somali/Horn of Africa populations carry MEFV mutations rarely documented in the literature.
regional_context: FMF is described primarily in Mediterranean and Middle Eastern populations but Somali ancestry shares historical population overlap. Profound underdiagnosis in Africa due to lack of genetic testing access.
key_clues: Episodic self-resolving fevers with complete recovery between episodes
Positive family history (brother with similar attacks)
Sterile peritonitis pattern — negative cultures consistently
red_herrings: Somalia malaria/TB endemic context
Abdominal pain during fevers suggesting infection
Age of onset at 53 (FMF usually childhood onset, but late onset documented)
source: First documented FMF case in Somalia, reconstructed from FMF literature
diagnosis_delay: 4 years (this specific case; global average 8-15 years in non-endemic populations)
source_title: Familial Mediterranean fever outside the Mediterranean: first documented case in Somalia

wrong_reasoning: Acute onset paralysis in a child during a potential outbreak — AFP surveillance protocol triggered. Polio was the first concern given the acute onset and the age group.
correct_reasoning: Chronic dietary cyanide exposure from improperly processed bitter cassava, combined with sulfur amino acid deficiency, causes irreversible upper motor neuron damage. Hyperreflexia and spasticity (NOT flaccid paralysis) is the critical discriminator from polio.
regional_context: Konzo is endemic in the cassava belt of Central Africa, particularly in Kwango, DRC, during drought and food insecurity periods. Estimated thousands of cases, mostly undiagnosed or misdiagnosed as polio.
key_clues: SPASTIC paraparesis with hyperreflexia — NOT flaccid (excludes polio)
Cluster of cases in same village during drought
Exclusive bitter cassava diet without proper soaking
red_herrings: Acute onset paralysis in a child triggering polio AFP protocol
Outbreak setting suggesting infectious etiology
Age and geography fitting polio demographic
source: Reconstructed from DRC Konzo outbreak reports — WHO and PAHO documentation
diagnosis_delay: Days to weeks for cluster recognition; months for sporadic cases
source_title: Konzo: a neurological disease associated with cyanide intoxication from cassava in sub-Saharan Africa

wrong_reasoning: Massive bilateral lower limb lymphedema in Ethiopia where LF is endemic. Patient received MDA albendazole/ivermectin as part of national LF control program.
correct_reasoning: Podoconiosis is caused by chronic transdermal absorption of irritant silicate minerals from volcanic red clay soils at altitudes above 1500m. Bilateral foot-upward pattern and negative filarial tests with highland soil exposure confirm the diagnosis. Family clustering reflects shared soil exposure.
regional_context: Ethiopia has the highest global burden of podoconiosis. Hawella Tula is a known hotspot. Altitude and soil type are the key geographic discriminators from LF.
key_clues: Altitude >1500m with volcanic red clay soil
Bilateral ascending from foot — classic podoconiosis pattern
red_herrings: Ethiopia LF endemicity driving MDA treatment
Bilateral lymphedema clinically identical to LF
Slightly elevated IgE (non-specific)
source: Reconstructed from podoconiosis literature — Davey et al. Ethiopia
diagnosis_delay: Years to over a decade (onset in first decade, most diagnosed in third decade or later)
source_title: Podoconiosis: a neglected tropical disease with a high burden in Ethiopia

wrong_reasoning: Safari visit to Zimbabwe wildlife park, fever, eschar-like skin lesion — African Tick Bite Fever is extremely common in this context. The lesion was interpreted as a tick bite eschar rather than a trypanosome chancre.
correct_diagnosis: Rhodesiense Human African Trypanosomiasis (East African Sleeping Sickness)
correct_reasoning: Rhodesiense HAT is a rapidly progressive zoonosis transmitted by tsetse fly in game parks. The skin chancre at bite site distinguishes it from tick bite fever eschar. Speed of CNS progression (within 2 weeks) is characteristic of rhodesiense vs. gambiense. Urgent suramin/melarsoprol treatment required.
regional_context: The Zambezi Valley is an active rhodesiense HAT focus. Wildlife safari tourists are at significant risk. Speed of progression is the critical clinical differentiator.
key_clues: Tsetse fly habitat (wildlife park, Zambezi Valley)
Chancre at bite site (softer, more defined than tick eschar)
red_herrings: Safari context strongly suggesting tick bite fever (very common)
Skin lesion resembling tick eschar
Fever + headache + myalgia fitting Rickettsia profile
source: Reconstructed from Sleeping Sickness case reports — Zimbabwe/Zambezi Valley
diagnosis_delay: 5 days misdiagnosed before correct dx (rHAT progresses to CNS within 2 weeks — any delay is life-threatening)
source_title: Rhodesiense sleeping sickness in travellers and tourists: systematic review

wrong_reasoning: Young female, butterfly facial rash, positive ANA 1:320, elevated ESR/CRP, polyarthralgia — meets multiple SLE classification criteria. ANA positivity in leprosy was not considered.
correct_reasoning: Leprosy is the 'great simulant' — induces ANA and RF production mimicking autoimmune disease. Worsening under immunosuppression, progressive peripheral neuropathy with anesthetic patches, madarosis, and thickened nerves are the discriminating features. MDT (rifampicin + dapsone + clofazimine) required urgently.
regional_context: KwaZulu-Natal has one of South Africa's highest leprosy burdens. Young women frequently misdiagnosed with SLE due to autoantibody cross-reactivity.
key_clues: Worsening skin lesions under immunosuppression — major red flag
red_herrings: ANA 1:320 positive (classic SLE marker — but also seen in leprosy)
Butterfly facial rash
Young female demographics fitting SLE profile
Polyarthralgia fitting SLE
source: Reconstructed from multibacillary leprosy misdiagnosis literature — South Africa
diagnosis_delay: 6 years (this specific case; mean 2.9 years in Colombian series of leprosy misdiagnosed as SLE)
source_title: Leprosy masquerading as systemic lupus erythematosus: diagnostic pitfalls

wrong_diagnosis: Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia (CIN) / possible cervical cancer
wrong_reasoning: Post-coital bleeding, abnormal colposcopic findings with vascular changes, and a 'suspicious' Pap smear in a resource-limited setting triggered concern for cervical cancer. Pathologist unfamiliar with FGS colposcopic appearance.
correct_reasoning: S. haematobium eggs deposited in genital mucosa cause granulomatous inflammation producing grainy sandy patches on colposcopy. Freshwater exposure history in endemic region is the key epidemiological clue. FGS affects ~40 million women in sub-Saharan Africa.
regional_context: Northern Nigeria and Jigawa State are highly endemic for S. haematobium due to irrigation agriculture. FGS is massively underdiagnosed and frequently confused with cervical neoplasia.
key_clues: Freshwater exposure history in S. haematobium endemic region
'Grainy sandy patches' on colposcopy — pathognomonic for FGS
Urine microscopy showing S. haematobium eggs
red_herrings: Post-coital bleeding + abnormal colposcopy strongly suggesting cervical cancer
'Suspicious' Pap smear result
Abnormal cervical blood vessels on colposcopy
source: Reconstructed from FGS literature — Kjetland et al., Nigeria
diagnosis_delay: Years to decades (FGS onset in childhood, rarely diagnosed in adulthood; 56 million affected, ~15,000 ever formally assessed)
source_title: Female genital schistosomiasis: a forgotten cause of morbidity in sub-Saharan Africa

wrong_reasoning: Young patient, no viral markers, liver failure — AIH initiated empirically. Steroids started.
correct_diagnosis: Wilson's Disease (Hepatic Presentation with Acute Liver Failure)
correct_reasoning: Wilson's Disease (autosomal recessive ATP7B mutation) causes copper accumulation in liver, brain, and cornea. In acute liver failure, the combination of Coombs-negative hemolysis + normal/low ALP + elevated urine copper is pathognomonic. Consanguinity and sibling neurological involvement support the genetic diagnosis.
regional_context: Wilson's Disease is severely underdiagnosed in East Africa due to lack of ceruloplasmin and copper assays. Consanguineous marriages in some Tanzanian communities increase autosomal recessive disease burden.
key_clues: Discordantly NORMAL alkaline phosphatase in acute liver failure — most important clue
red_herrings: No viral hepatitis markers making AIH the default
Young age fitting AIH demographic
Steroids given empirically delaying copper chelation
source: Reconstructed from Wilson's Disease case literature in East Africa
diagnosis_delay: 22.5 months global mean; up to 12 years in African case series
source_title: Wilson disease in Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis

wrong_reasoning: Positive Mantoux test, lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis, elevated protein, North Africa TB burden, persistent headache with papilledema — classical TB meningitis presentation triggered anti-TB therapy.
correct_diagnosis: Neuro-Behcet Disease with Cerebral Venous Thrombosis
correct_reasoning: Behcet's Disease is a systemic vasculitis with highest prevalence along the Silk Road, including North Africa. CVT in a young North African male with recurrent mucosal ulcers should always trigger Behcet's workup. The oral and genital ulcers are the diagnostic key, often dismissed as unrelated.
regional_context: Tunisia has significantly elevated Behcet's Disease prevalence compared to global rates. HLA-B51 frequency is high in North African Arab populations. Often misdiagnosed as TB meningitis or idiopathic intracranial hypertension.
key_clues: Recurrent oral ulcers in history (often dismissed)
CVT in a young male — unusual, vasculitis etiology
red_herrings: Positive Mantoux in TB-endemic North Africa
Lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis fitting TB meningitis
Elevated CSF protein
Oral ulcers dismissed as aphthous/unrelated
source: Reconstructed from Behcet's Disease case reports in North Africa
diagnosis_delay: 4.5 years (mean 53.5 months in Tunisian neuro-Behcet cohort before CVT diagnosed)
source_title: Neuro-Behcet disease in Tunisia: clinical features, diagnosis and outcome
